_analysis.relevance_explanation: This technology could significantly enhance the capability of AI agents to operate autonomously in digital markets, potentially disrupting existing platform dependencies and competitive dynamics.
_analysis.market_signal: Advanced AI memory systems may enable agents to perform complex tasks independently, reducing reliance on dominant platforms like Google or Amazon for information retrieval and decision-making.

items.title: Decentralized Multi-Agent Systems with Shared Context
items.abstract: Multi-agent systems (MAS) can scale large language model reasoning at test time by decomposing complex problems into parallel subtasks. However, most existing MAS rely on centralized orchestration, where a main agent assigns work, collects outputs, and merges results. As the number of subtasks grows, this controller becomes a communication and integration bottleneck. We propose Decentralized Language Models (DeLM), a MAS framework that decentralizes coordination through parallel agents, a shared verified context, and a task queue. Agents asynchronously claim subtasks, read accumulated progress, perform local reasoning, and write back compact verified updates. The shared context acts as a common communication substrate, enabling agents to build on one another's verified progress without routing every update through a central controller. Empirically, DeLM improves both software-engineering test-time scaling and long-context reasoning. On SWE-bench Verified, DeLM achieves the best performance across Avg.@1, Pass@2, and Pass@4, with gains of up to 10.5 percentage points over the strongest baseline, while reducing cost per task by roughly 50%. On LongBench-v2 Multi-Doc QA, DeLM achieves

_analysis.summary: This paper proposes a new framework called Decentralized Language Models (DeLM) for coordinating large language model (LLM) agents without a central controller. Instead of a single agent assigning tasks, multiple agents work in parallel, share a verified context, and pull subtasks from a queue asynchronously, reducing bottlenecks in large-scale problem-solving.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: Decentralized multi-agent systems could reduce reliance on dominant AI platforms (e.g., those controlling centralized orchestration), potentially lowering barriers to entry and shifting market power toward smaller or open-source alternatives.
_analysis.market_signal: This signals a potential shift toward decentralized AI coordination, which may challenge the dominance of vertically integrated AI platforms by enabling more modular and competitive ecosystems.

items.title: Accounting for AI Inference in Corporate GHG Inventories: A Four-Tier Methodology for Scope 3 Category 1 Reporting
items.abstract: AI inference services -- API subscriptions, enterprise chat tools, and SaaS products with embedded AI features -- fall unambiguously within Scope 3 Category 1 under the Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD), which requires disclosure for fiscal years starting January 2024. Yet no standardised methodology exists for including them in corporate GHG inventories. Current practice either omits the category entirely or applies a generic economic input-output (EEIO) factor calibrated to the ICT sector as a whole, overestimating AI inference emissions by 10-40x relative to physically derived alternatives. We propose a four-tier framework that matches estimation precision to the data organisations can realistically obtain, progressing from direct token-based physical estimation -- using GPU energy benchmarks and regional grid carbon intensities -- down to a spend-based EEIO fallback for services where no usage data exists. Emission factors are derived from peer-reviewed GPU energy benchmarks (ML.ENERGY Leaderboard v3), confirmed grid carbon intensities (EPA eGRID 2023; Ember 2023), and published water use effectiveness data (Li et al., 2025). Applied to a 200-person European f

_analysis.summary: This paper argues that AI inference services (like API subscriptions or AI-powered SaaS tools) must be included in corporate greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions reporting under the EU’s Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD), which took effect in 2024. It proposes a four-tier methodology to accurately measure these emissions, criticizing current practices that overestimate AI’s carbon footprint by 10-40 times due to reliance on outdated industry averages.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: While the paper focuses on environmental reporting, its call for standardized AI emissions accounting could indirectly influence digital competition policy by shaping how regulators assess the environmental costs of AI services, which may affect market entry and innovation incentives.
_analysis.market_signal: The paper signals growing scrutiny over AI’s environmental impact, which could lead to stricter reporting requirements and potentially alter competitive dynamics in the AI services market.

items.title: Post-Quantum Secure Federated DeFi for Inclusive Banking
items.abstract: Recent advances in error-corrected qubits have accelerated the timeline for practical quantum computing. It poses a threat to cryptographic primitives used to secure financial systems, government infrastructure, communication networks, and DeFi (Decentralized Finance) ecosystems. This paper introduces a post-quantum secure federated DeFi framework that enables inter-bank collaboration to improve the inclusivity of individuals underserved by local lenders due to limited financial histories. Multiple banks contribute encrypted information batches to a virtual server, where lattice-based Fully Homomorphic Encryption (FHE) enables end-to-end homomorphic computation. The server fuses local data-driven probabilistic assessments, expert beliefs, and verifiable evidence generated by the NASA-IBM Prithvi Geospatial Foundation Model (GFM), in encrypted format. Decentralized technologies are employed to ensure tamper-proof evidence and auditable accountability for all encrypted data exchanges between institutions and the server. The framework is tested on agricultural lending decisions for rural borrowers in Virginia.

_analysis.summary: This paper warns that practical quantum computing could break current encryption methods, threatening secure financial systems like DeFi (Decentralized Finance, a blockchain-based financial system without central authorities). It proposes a post-quantum secure federated DeFi framework using lattice-based cryptography (a quantum-resistant encryption method) to enable banks to collaborate securely while improving financial inclusion for underserved individuals.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: The paper highlights a looming technological disruption (quantum computing) that could undermine the security foundations of digital financial markets, potentially reshaping competitive dynamics in DeFi and banking.
_analysis.market_signal: Quantum-resistant cryptography may become a critical infrastructure requirement for digital financial markets, creating new competitive advantages for firms that adopt it early.

items.title: Dynamic Linear Attention
items.abstract: The scalability of Large Language Models (LLMs) to long contexts is fundamentally constrained by the quadratic complexity of standard attention, motivating the adoption of linear attention mechanisms with sub-quadratic cost. To improve representation capacity under long contexts, recent approaches organize memory in a multi-state manner. However, existing multi-state linear attention methods rely on fixed state merging policies that cannot adapt to dynamically varying token importance, irreversibly obscuring critical tokens and causing severe error accumulation over long sequences. To address this limitation, we propose DLA, a dynamic memory modeling framework for multi-state linear attention. DLA introduces (i) Information-Aware Dynamic State Merging, which adaptively determines state boundaries based on token-level information variation, preserving high-resolution representations around semantic transitions while aggressively summarizing stable regions, and (ii) Capacity-Bounded Memory Modeling, which maintains a fixed-size, chronologically ordered state cache by selectively merging adjacent low-information states to control memory growth with minimal information loss. We pre-tra

_analysis.summary: This paper examines the limitations of current 'linear attention' mechanisms in Large Language Models (LLMs), which are designed to handle long text sequences more efficiently than traditional methods. It highlights a problem where fixed memory policies in these models can permanently lose important information in long texts, leading to errors. The authors propose a new approach called 'Dynamic Linear Attention' to better adapt to changing text importance.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: This paper signals how advances in AI model efficiency could reduce barriers to entry for smaller firms competing with dominant LLM providers, potentially reshaping the AI infrastructure market.
_analysis.market_signal: Improved LLM efficiency may lower costs for AI startups, challenging the dominance of major tech firms in the AI infrastructure and cloud services markets.

items.title: How Does Reasoning Flow? Tracing Attention-Induced Information Flow for Targeted RL in LLMs
items.abstract: Token-level credit assignment remains a key obstacle for reinforcement learning (RL) in large language models (LLMs), where RL recipes typically treat all tokens equally, failing to distinguish decisive reasoning steps from routine formatting or fluent filler. Recent attempts leverage model-internal signals to assign finer-grained credit, but these are often point-wise heuristics that ignore the global structure of information propagation. We propose FlowTracer, an RL framework that traces answer-targeted reasoning flow on an attention-induced directed acyclic graph in which nodes correspond to tokens and edge capacities come from aggregated attention weights and derives token credit from this global structure. The edge capacities are reweighted to retain only the influence that can reach the answer region, while enforcing local flow conservation so intermediate tokens neither lose nor gain effective mass due to path length or irrelevant branches. On this graph, FlowTracer extracts an information-flow backbone connecting the question to the answer and scores tokens by flow throughput, revealing high-impact hubs and aggregation checkpoints that mediate long-range dependencies. These

_analysis.summary: This paper introduces FlowTracer, a new reinforcement learning (RL) framework for large language models (LLMs) that improves how the model assigns credit to different parts of its reasoning process. Instead of treating all parts of a response equally, it tracks how information flows through the model's attention mechanisms to identify which steps are most important for generating the final answer.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: This work highlights how AI models can be optimized to improve reasoning efficiency, which may influence the competitive dynamics of AI-powered services by enabling more precise and controllable outputs.
_analysis.market_signal: Advances in LLM reasoning could lead to more specialized AI services, potentially disrupting markets where precise, explainable outputs are critical (e.g., legal, medical, or financial advisory tools).

items.title: STORM: Stepwise Token Optimization with Reward-Guided Beam Search
items.abstract: Modern retrieval increasingly relies on dense and learned-sparse neural models that are effective but require encoding the entire corpus into a specialized index, rebuilt whenever the model changes. Lexical retrievers like BM25 stay efficient and transparent on a standard inverted index that need not change as models evolve, but suffer from vocabulary mismatch. LLM query rewriting can help, yet prompted rewriters emit well-formed but retrieval-ineffective or harmful-terms, and training against a retrieval reward gives only delayed, sequence-level supervision that obscures which terms helped. We introduce STORM (Stepwise Token Optimization with Reward-guided beaM search), a self-supervised framework for lexical query expansion. STORM trains the rewriter through generation guided by retrieval metrics: at each step, candidate expansions are scored against the BM25 index and low-reward continuations pruned, turning the retrieval reward into a token-level signal that concentrates exploration on retrieval-effective vocabulary. Across TREC DL and BEIR, STORM lets 0.6B-8B backbones match or surpass competitive LLM rewriters while retrieving as fast as plain BM25; at 8B it rivals far larger

_analysis.summary: This paper proposes STORM, a new method for improving how search systems retrieve information by optimizing search queries step-by-step using rewards from retrieval performance. It highlights challenges with current search models, such as the need to rebuild indexes when models change and the ineffectiveness of traditional lexical retrievers like BM25 due to vocabulary mismatches. The authors argue that training search systems with retrieval rewards can lead to better query rewriting, though current methods provide delayed feedback that obscures which terms are most effective.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: This paper signals how advances in AI-driven search optimization could disrupt existing search engine monopolies by enabling more efficient and adaptive retrieval methods, potentially reducing barriers to entry for competitors.
_analysis.market_signal: AI-powered query optimization techniques like STORM may erode the dominance of established search engines by improving the effectiveness of smaller or newer competitors' retrieval systems.

items.title: Can Image Models Imagine Time? ImageTime: A Novel Benchmark for Probing Visual World Modeling Through Spatiotemporal Consistency
items.abstract: Image generation models now produce high-quality static images, yet their ability to represent how a visual world changes over time remains poorly understood. Practical workflows such as storyboarding, step-by-step illustration, reference-guided editing, and video previsualization require models to preserve identities, objects, spatial relations, and causal order across multiple visual states. Existing evaluations largely measure single-image correctness, compositional alignment, or video quality, leaving open whether an image model can coherently imagine a temporally ordered process. We introduce ImageTime, a diagnostic benchmark that uses spatiotemporal consistency as a behavioral probe of visual world modeling in image generation. Given an action instruction, and optionally a reference image specifying the initial state, a model must generate one image containing four ordered key states: initial state, action onset, transition state, and final state. This four-keyframe protocol is more temporally demanding than single-image generation while avoiding the confounds of dense video dynamics. ImageTime organizes tasks with a progressive capability hierarchy and decomposes each scenar

_analysis.summary: This paper introduces a new benchmark called ImageTime to test whether AI image generation models can understand and represent changes over time in visual scenes. Current models excel at creating static images but struggle to maintain consistency in objects, identities, and causal order across multiple generated frames—a skill essential for applications like video editing or storyboarding. The authors argue that existing evaluations don’t measure this spatiotemporal coherence, leaving a gap in understanding how these models might disrupt creative workflows or content generation markets.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: This work reveals a critical limitation in AI image models that could shape competitive dynamics in creative industries, where temporal consistency is a key differentiator for high-quality content generation tools.
_analysis.market_signal: AI models that master spatiotemporal consistency could accelerate the obsolescence of traditional creative tools, threatening incumbents like Adobe or Canva and enabling new entrants to challenge their dominance.

items.title: Learning What to Remember: Observability-Safe Memory Retention via Constrained Optimization for Long-Horizon Language Agents
items.abstract: Long-horizon language agents accumulate observations, reasoning traces, and retrieved facts that exceed their finite context windows, making memory retention a fundamental resource-allocation problem. Existing memory systems improve management through heuristic scoring, retrieval optimization, or learned compression, but largely treat retention as a local decision problem and do not explicitly model its long-term consequences under realistic observability constraints. To fill this gap, we formulate memory retention as a constrained stochastic optimization problem with explicit budget feasibility, evidence utility, and delayed costs including miss penalties, reacquisition delays, and stale-information risk. We then propose OSL-MR (Observability-Safe Learning for Memory Retention), a novel framework that enforces a strict separation between online-observable features and offline-available supervision (OAS). OSL-MR combines an evidence learner trained from realized evidence supervision with a Mixed-Score heuristic that serves both as a deployable online-safe baseline and as a structured inductive prior for learning. The resulting policy learns query-conditioned evidence value directly

_analysis.summary: This paper proposes a new method for managing memory in AI agents, which are systems that use artificial intelligence to perform tasks over long periods by storing and retrieving information. The authors frame memory retention as a constrained optimization problem, ensuring AI agents do not exceed their memory limits while retaining the most useful information. A 'language agent' is an AI system that processes and generates human-like text to accomplish goals.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: This work signals how AI agents could disrupt digital markets by improving efficiency in memory-intensive tasks, potentially reducing barriers to entry for competitors in sectors like search, customer service, or content moderation.
_analysis.market_signal: Advances in AI memory optimization may enable new entrants to compete more effectively with dominant platforms by reducing reliance on proprietary data storage and retrieval systems.

items.title: Dexterous Point Policy: Learning Point-based Dexterous Hand Policies from Human Demonstrations
items.abstract: Robotic foundation models pre-trained on human demonstration videos have shown promise, but a significant embodiment gap remains when the resulting policies are deployed on real robots. A common remedy is to fine-tune these models on robot-specific demonstrations. However, robot data collection can be prohibitively expensive and time-consuming, which is particularly acute in dexterous manipulation, e.g., teleoperating a multi-fingered hand for even a single atomic task can take days. To address this, we introduce Dexterous Point Policy, a framework that learns dexterous manipulation policies directly from human videos and requires no robot demonstrations. Our core insight is that a unified 3D keypoint representation can bridge human and robot embodiments when used for both observations and actions. Specifically, we extract 3D keypoints of task-relevant objects and human hands from raw videos, and train an autoregressive transformer over these keypoints. We observe that at the keypoint level, specifically the wrist and fingertips, human and robot behaviors closely align, enabling direct policy transfer. On a suite of real-robot tasks spanning pick-and-place and tool use, Dexterous P

_analysis.summary: This paper introduces Dexterous Point Policy, a new method to train robotic hand policies (AI systems controlling multi-fingered robotic hands) using human demonstration videos instead of expensive robot-specific data. The goal is to reduce the 'embodiment gap'—the difference between simulated or human-trained AI and real-world robotic performance—without requiring costly teleoperation (manual control of robots for data collection).
_analysis.relevance_explanation: While the paper focuses on robotics, its broader implication is that AI systems can be trained more efficiently using human data, which may reduce barriers to entry for robotics firms and challenge incumbent players in automation markets.
_analysis.market_signal: The development signals that AI training methods may become more data-efficient, potentially lowering costs for new entrants in robotics and automation, which could disrupt existing market structures in industrial AI.

items.title: Geometry-Aware Reinforcement Learning for 2D Irregular Nesting
items.abstract: Traditional heuristic solvers for the 2D irregular nesting problem share a fundamental limitation: they are blind to polygon geometry, relying on guided brute-force to navigate the continuous placement space with minimal geometrical guidance. In this paper, we argue that Reinforcement Learning is uniquely positioned to overcome this bottleneck. By pairing an optimization policy with a geometry-aware neural encoder, an agent can automatically discover rich geometric priors directly from data, utilizing these learned intuitions to strategically guide exploration. To realize this, we introduce the Polygons Transformer (PoT), a novel architecture that encodes 2D continuous vector geometries while allowing cross-polygons attention. We couple this novel architecture with a Combinatorial Optimization Reinforcement Learning (CORL) training framework to find optimal solutions. To support this paradigm, we release an open-source training dataset derived from complex geographic contours alongside a dedicated evaluation benchmark. Our empirical validation demonstrates that our trained agent achieves area utilization performance highly competitive with Sparrow, the state-of-the-art heuristic so

_analysis.summary: This paper proposes using reinforcement learning (a type of AI where agents learn by trial and error) to solve the 2D irregular nesting problem—a task in manufacturing where shapes (like pieces of fabric or metal) must be arranged efficiently on a flat surface. The authors argue that traditional methods struggle because they don’t account for the geometric properties of the shapes, but their AI approach learns these properties automatically to improve efficiency.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: This paper signals how AI can optimize complex industrial processes, which may indirectly affect digital markets by improving the efficiency of firms that rely on such optimization (e.g., logistics, supply chains, or manufacturing platforms).
_analysis.market_signal: AI-driven optimization tools could reduce operational costs for firms, potentially strengthening their competitive positions in digital markets where efficiency is a key differentiator.

items.title: Causal Ensemble Agent: Hierarchical Causal Discovery with LLM-guided Expert Reweighting
items.abstract: Causal discovery aims to uncover causal structures from observational data, which is crucial for real-world decision-making. However, different causal discovery algorithms can produce divergent results that conflict with each other, complicating the identification of accurate causal graphs. Traditional approaches rely on numerical values and statistical assumptions, often ignoring rich domain-specific information, such as feature descriptions, which could also help structure learning. While recent works explore using Large Language Models (LLMs) to infer causal relations via direct queries, such methods can be unreliable due to a lack of alignment with the actual data. To address these limitations, we propose Causal Ensemble Agent (CEA), a novel framework that aggregates structural insights from statistical discovery experts across different graph levels via linear opinion pooling, and uses an LLM as a meta-referee to dynamically reweight experts when the aggregated confidence is close to the decision boundary, thereby composing an improved and more complete causal graph. Extensive experiments on both synthetic and real-world datasets demonstrate that CEA achieves the strongest ove

_analysis.summary: This paper introduces a new method called the Causal Ensemble Agent, which combines multiple causal discovery algorithms (methods to find cause-and-effect relationships in data) with guidance from large language models (LLMs) to improve accuracy. Traditional methods often produce conflicting results, but this approach uses feature descriptions (text descriptions of data) to refine the learning process, addressing gaps in purely numerical or statistical approaches.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: This work signals a shift toward AI-driven causal inference that could reduce uncertainty in digital market analysis, potentially empowering regulators to detect anti-competitive behavior more precisely.
_analysis.market_signal: AI-enhanced causal discovery tools may enable regulators or competitors to uncover hidden market power or anti-competitive practices in digital ecosystems more efficiently.

items.title: Dmsh: A Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning Framework for All-Quad Mesh Generation
items.abstract: Generating high-quality meshes for arbitrary geometries remains a fundamental bottleneck in computational engineering, often demanding heuristic tuning and semi-manual workflows. In this paper, we introduce Dmsh, a first fully automated reinforcement learning pipeline that unifies geometric decomposition and quadrilateral mesh generation within a single learning-based framework. Dmsh decomposes the problem through three coordinated agents handling topology simplification, geometric regularization, and mesh generation. The meshing process is formulated as a Markov Decision Process and solved using a parametric Soft Actor-Critic architecture with decoupled critics, enabling efficient exploration of a hybrid discrete-continuous action space. A curriculum learning strategy ensures scalability from simple domains to highly complex geometries, suppressing seed variance. By design, the recursive decomposition enables parallel meshing of subregions, yielding globally conforming all-quadrilateral meshes without post hoc correction. Across a wide range of benchmarks, Dmsh consistently outperforms existing methods in automation, robustness, and mesh quality, establishing a new paradigm for le

_analysis.summary: This paper introduces Dmsh, an automated AI system that uses multiple reinforcement learning agents to generate high-quality quadrilateral meshes for complex geometries without manual intervention. The system breaks down the task into three coordinated agents: one for simplifying geometry, another for regularizing shapes, and a third for mesh generation, all working together within a single learning framework.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: While Dmsh is a technical advancement in computational engineering, it does not directly address digital competition policy but signals the growing sophistication of AI-driven automation in specialized technical domains.
_analysis.market_signal: The automation of complex engineering tasks like mesh generation may reduce reliance on specialized software tools, potentially disrupting niche markets for computational design software.

items.title: Towards Diverse Scientific Hypothesis Search with Large Language Models
items.abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are on the rise for accelerating scientific discovery, most recently in advanced tasks such as generating valid scientific hypotheses. Yet in many discovery settings, the goal is not to identify a single best hypothesis since validation can be noisy and expensive, and scientists benefit from a set of high-quality alternative hypotheses that hedge against downstream uncertainty for the best solutions. Nevertheless, commonly used evolutionary search recipes tend to prioritize optimization over exploration in hypothesis generation, and the resulting selection pressure during the search process leads to diversity collapse. Motivated by these limitations, we formulate hypothesis search as a sampling problem, where the objective is to efficiently produce diverse, high-quality hypotheses under a fixed validation budget. Building on this perspective, we propose \ours, an evolutionary framework inspired by the classical parallel tempering algorithm that searches hypotheses at multiple temperature levels and enables principled information exchange across temperatures to improve exploration without disrupting convergence. Across domains including molecular discove

_analysis.summary: This paper explores how large language models (LLMs) can be used to generate diverse scientific hypotheses, addressing a gap in current AI-driven discovery methods that often prioritize single 'best' solutions over a range of high-quality alternatives. The authors argue that exploration (generating varied options) is as important as optimization (refining the best option) in hypothesis generation, which is critical for fields where validation is costly and uncertain.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: This paper signals a shift toward AI-driven diversification in knowledge generation, which could disrupt traditional market structures where a few dominant players control hypothesis validation (e.g., academic journals, R&D labs) and reduce barriers to entry for alternative research pathways.
_analysis.market_signal: LLMs enabling diverse hypothesis generation may reduce reliance on centralized research institutions, potentially fragmenting knowledge production and challenging incumbents' gatekeeping roles in scientific discovery.

items.title: NOVA: Symbolic Regression Discovery of Interpretable Car-Following and Lane-Change Models with Driver Heterogeneity
items.abstract: We present NOVA, an autonomous symbolic regression framework that identifies interpretable car-following and lane-change structures from raw trajectory data with minimal behavioral priors. Applied to 4,765,788 active driving observations from the NGSIM I-80 and US-101 datasets, NOVA's deterministic Rust-powered search engine evaluates over 10,000 candidate algebraic structures and identifies a compact two-term acceleration model under a forward-shifted rolling-mean prediction target. Evaluated under two complementary preprocessing pipelines, NOVA achieves $RMSE = 1.376 m/s^2$ ($R^2 = 15.57\%$) on the intent-forecasting benchmark, outperforming the best recalibrated symbolic-regression baseline (SR-LLM, PNAS~2025) by 0.135 m/s$^2$ in RMSE under an identical evaluation protocol. Across eight independent experiments, a single dominant nonlinear term emerges as a robust backbone of human car-following; a residual-guided extension further links the selected structure to an established psychophysical theory of collision avoidance. The discovered feature operators transfer zero-shot between freeway sites with under 3 pp $R^2$ loss. Extended to lane-change modelling within a multinomial lo

_analysis.summary: NOVA is an AI tool that analyzes driving behavior by automatically discovering mathematical models for how cars follow each other and change lanes, using real-world traffic data. It found a simple two-term formula that predicts acceleration with moderate accuracy, suggesting human driving can be captured by compact, interpretable rules rather than black-box AI.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: While the paper focuses on traffic modeling, its use of symbolic regression to derive interpretable, compact models from large datasets signals how AI could similarly uncover hidden competitive dynamics in digital markets.
_analysis.market_signal: AI-driven discovery of interpretable market models may reduce reliance on opaque algorithms, potentially increasing transparency in digital competition analysis.

items.title: Drawing with Strangers: Population Scaling Drives Zero-Shot Mutual Intelligibility in Emergent Sketching
items.abstract: Generalization in emergent communication has largely focused on novel inputs or linguistic structures, yet the capacity for agents to communicate with strangers from strictly disjoint communities remains relatively unexplored. In this work, we formalize this capability as \textit{zero-shot mutual intelligibility (ZMI)}: successful communication between independently trained populations without prior exposure. Leveraging emergent sketching -- in which agents communicate through sets of drawn strokes -- as a visually grounded modality, we find that scaling the training population substantially improves ZMI across independent groups. Crucially, as we scale the population size, in-group communicative variation increases, preventing co-adaptation into homogeneity. Simultaneously, cross-group variation decreases, indicating a structural convergence toward a certain type of universality. Further analysis reveals that this universality is achieved through perceptual grounding: scaled populations increasingly anchor their emergent sketches on the objective visual resemblance of the target images. Together, these results position ZMI as a distinct axis of generalization in emergent communica

_analysis.summary: This academic paper studies how AI agents trained separately can communicate with each other without prior interaction, a concept called *zero-shot mutual intelligibility (ZMI)*. The researchers use a sketch-based communication system where agents draw strokes to convey meaning, finding that larger training populations improve this cross-population communication ability.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: Demonstrates how AI agents can achieve interoperability without shared training, signaling potential for decentralized or fragmented AI ecosystems that could disrupt gatekeeper-controlled platforms.
_analysis.market_signal: AI systems may increasingly communicate across independent deployments, reducing reliance on centralized platforms and challenging their control over data flows.

items.title: One Token per Multimodal Evidence: Latent Memory for Resource-Constrained QA
items.abstract: External memory effectively grounds large language models (LLMs) and vision-language models (VLMs)-based question answering (QA) in relevant multimodal evidence. However, existing memory paradigms represent each memory item in raw text and image forms, so retrieval-based systems must pass the retrieved text or images to the generation LLMs/VLMs, resulting in high token consumption and storage pressure, making it unaffordable for resource-constrained applications. We propose Latent Memory, a latent-space memory paradigm that replaces each raw text or image evidence item with a single high-dimensional latent token produced by a small compressor LLM/VLM. Rather than retrieving raw evidence for generation, Latent Memory operates in a unified latent representation space: the query is embedded into this space to retrieve relevant latent tokens, and the retrieved latent tokens are directly prompted to a pretrained LLM or VLM for answer generation. To make each latent token simultaneously informative for reconstruction, retrieval, and generation, we train the compressor with reconstruction, contrastive, and distillation objectives in a unified end-to-end manner. Latent Memory is evaluated

_analysis.summary: This paper introduces *Latent Memory*, a new method for improving how AI models (like large language models or vision-language models) store and retrieve multimodal evidence (text and images) for question answering. Instead of storing raw data, it compresses each piece of evidence into a single high-dimensional token, reducing storage and processing costs for resource-constrained applications.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: This technology signals potential shifts in the cost structures and efficiency of AI-powered services, which could influence market entry barriers and competitive dynamics in digital markets where AI-driven QA systems are critical.
_analysis.market_signal: More efficient AI memory systems could lower barriers to entry for smaller firms competing with large tech platforms in AI-driven question-answering and multimodal services.

items.title: Improving Adversarial Transferability on Vision-Language Pre-training Models via Surrogate-Specific Bias Correction
items.abstract: Adversarial examples reveal vulnerabilities in Vision-Language Pre-training (VLP) models and provide insights for improving robustness. A key property is cross-model transferability, which enables transfer-based black-box attacks. However, existing attacks often rely heavily on the surrogate model, causing cross-model performance drops. One reason is that adversarial optimization may follow surrogate model responses more than input semantics, making the update direction effective on the surrogate but less transferable to unseen targets. We refer to this dependency as surrogate-specific bias. Motivated by this observation, DeBias-Attack improves transferability by correcting surrogate-specific bias in adversarial optimization directions. It maintains two perturbation branches. The main branch optimizes a perturbation on the original image and obtains the adversarial gradient used to disrupt image-text alignment. The reference branch optimizes a perturbation on a weak-semantic image constructed from the dataset mean image with small Gaussian noise resampled at each iteration. Since this weak-semantic image contains little clear visual content, its optimization reflects surrogate resp

_analysis.summary: This paper studies how adversarial attacks—deliberate manipulations of AI models—can exploit weaknesses in Vision-Language Pre-training (VLP) models, which combine image and text data. It finds that current attacks often rely too much on the 'surrogate model' (the AI model used to generate attacks), making them less effective against other models. The authors propose a method called 'surrogate-specific bias correction' to improve the transferability of these attacks across different models.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: While not directly about competition policy, this research highlights vulnerabilities in AI models that could affect the competitive dynamics of AI-powered services, particularly in markets where robustness and reliability are key differentiators.
_analysis.market_signal: The paper signals that AI model robustness is becoming a critical factor in the competitive landscape, potentially favoring firms with superior security or defense mechanisms.

items.title: Benchmarking Knowledge Editing using Logical Rules
items.abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed in real-world applications that require access to up-to-date knowledge. However, retraining LLMs is computationally expensive. Therefore, knowledge editing techniques are crucial for maintaining current information and correcting erroneous assertions within pre-trained models. Current benchmarks for knowledge editing primarily focus on recalling edited facts, often neglecting their logical consequences. To address this limitation, we introduce a new benchmark designed to evaluate how knowledge editing methods handle the logical consequences of a single fact edit. Our benchmark extracts relevant logical rules from a knowledge graph for a given edit. Then, it generates multi-hop questions based on these rules to assess the impact on logical consequences. Our findings indicate that while existing knowledge editing approaches can accurately insert direct assertions into LLMs, they frequently fail to inject entailed knowledge. Specifically, experiments with popular methods like ROME and FT reveal a substantial performance gap, up to 24%, between evaluations on directly edited knowledge and on entailed knowledge. This highlights the

_analysis.summary: This paper introduces a new benchmark to test how well AI models (called Large Language Models or LLMs) can update their knowledge while also understanding the logical consequences of those updates. Current methods only check if edited facts are recalled correctly, but this new approach evaluates whether the model can reason about related facts that should also change as a result.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: This work highlights how AI model updates can inadvertently introduce inconsistencies, which may affect competition in AI-powered services where accuracy and reliability are critical for market trust.
_analysis.market_signal: AI model editing capabilities could influence the reliability of AI-driven services, potentially shaping competitive dynamics in sectors like search, recommendation systems, or AI assistants.

items.title: ActiveMem: Distributed Active Memory for Long-Horizon LLM Reasoning
items.abstract: Memory is essential for enabling large language model (LLM) agents to handle long-horizon reasoning tasks. Existing memory mechanisms are largely centralized, typically organizing retrieved information and interaction history within a single model context. This design imposes a fundamental trade-off: scaling reasoning trajectories risks context overload, whereas aggressive content pruning may result in irreversible information loss. Seeking a better trade-off, we draw inspiration from human cognitive systems, especially the functional complementarity between the prefrontal cortex (executive control) and the hippocampus (memory management), suggesting that such a trade-off need not be inherent, but may instead stem from centralized memory organization. To this end, we propose ActiveMem, a heterogeneous framework that decouples agent memory from the core reasoning process. Specifically, a high-level Planner utilizes distilled semantic gists to execute reasoning, while a lightweight, distributed memory system operates in parallel to actively accumulate and consolidate these gists throughout the task. Experiments on BrowseComp-Plus and GAIA show that ActiveMem achieves state-of-the-art

_analysis.summary: This paper proposes a new memory system called ActiveMem for large language model (LLM) agents to improve long-horizon reasoning by distributing memory across multiple components instead of relying on a single centralized context. The authors argue this approach reduces the trade-off between context overload and information loss, drawing inspiration from human cognitive systems like the prefrontal cortex.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: This technology could enable AI agents to perform more complex tasks autonomously, potentially reducing reliance on dominant platforms like Google or Microsoft for search and productivity tools.
_analysis.market_signal: Distributed memory systems may accelerate the shift toward AI agents that operate independently, challenging the dominance of gatekeeper platforms in digital markets.

items.title: LC-QAT: Data-Efficient 2-Bit QAT for LLMs via Linear-Constrained Vector Quantization
items.abstract: Quantization-aware training (QAT) is essential for extremely low-bit large language models (LLMs). Current QAT methods are mainly based on scalar quantization (SQ), which enables efficient optimization but suffers from severe performance degradation at 2-bit precision. On the other hand, vector quantization (VQ) provides substantially higher representational capacity, but its discrete codebook lookup prevents end-to-end training. We propose LC-QAT, a 2-bit weight-only VQ-QAT framework that represents quantized weights via a learned affine mapping over discrete vectors, which yields a high-quality PTQ initialization and enables fully differentiable end-to-end optimization without explicit codebook lookup in the training forward pass. This strong post-training initialization makes LC-QAT highly data-efficient. Experiments across diverse LLMs demonstrate that LC-QAT consistently outperforms state-of-the-art QAT methods while using only 0.1%--10% of the training data. Our results establish LC-QAT as a practical and scalable solution for extreme low-bit model deployment.

_analysis.summary: This paper introduces LC-QAT, a new method for training large language models (LLMs) with extreme 2-bit precision using vector quantization (VQ), which improves performance compared to traditional scalar quantization (SQ). The technique uses a learned affine mapping over discrete vectors to enable end-to-end training while maintaining high representational capacity.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: While the paper focuses on technical improvements in LLM efficiency, it signals potential shifts in AI infrastructure markets where specialized hardware and training methods could become critical for competitive advantage.
_analysis.market_signal: The advancement of ultra-low-bit quantization methods may lower barriers to entry for smaller firms to develop and deploy competitive AI models, challenging incumbent tech giants' dominance in AI infrastructure.

items.title: Machine Learning Methods for Studying Latent Neural Activity Dynamics
items.abstract: Recent developments in brain recording are driving a demand for machine learning tools capable of decoding the latent structure of large populations of neurons. In this paper, we provide a comprehensive survey that outlines the trajectory of Latent Variable Models (LVMs) from early state-space models to more recent deep generative models. We organize the literature into three closely related domains: (1) Single-Region Latent Dynamics, which includes models such as linear dynamical systems to more complex dynamics represented by Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs) and Neural Ordinary Differential Equations (ODEs); (2) Multi-Region Communication, which employs probabilistic as well as subspace methods to study how information is transferred across different brain areas considering synaptic propagation delays and network connectivity; and (3) Behavior-Aligned Modeling, which seeks to disentangle neural activity related to task performance from other internal states via supervised or contrastive learning. This survey also includes large-scale neural foundation models, such as Transformers and diffusion models, that rely on large-scale pre-training for optimal performance across subjects.

_analysis.summary: This paper reviews advances in machine learning models, called Latent Variable Models (LVMs), used to decode complex patterns in neural activity data from brain recordings. It traces the evolution from simple statistical models to modern deep learning approaches like Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs) and Neural Ordinary Differential Equations (neural ODEs), which capture dynamic, high-dimensional data.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: While the paper focuses on neuroscience applications, its discussion of deep generative models and high-dimensional data analysis is relevant to AI-driven digital platforms and their competitive dynamics.
_analysis.market_signal: Advances in deep generative models for complex data analysis may accelerate AI capabilities in digital markets, potentially intensifying competition or enabling new forms of market power.

items.title: Assessing Automated Prompt Injection Attacks in Agentic Environments
items.abstract: Indirect prompt injection poses a critical threat to LLM agents that interact with untrusted external data, yet automated attack methods--proven effective for jailbreaking--remain underexplored in realistic agentic settings. We present a comprehensive empirical evaluation of automated prompt injection attacks against LLM agents, adapting both white-box (GCG) and black-box (TAP) methods to the agentic setting within the AgentDojo framework. We evaluate across 80 task pairs spanning four domains and multiple models, and find that black-box optimization substantially outperforms gradient-based methods, a gap we attribute to GCG's optimization instability under reasonable compute budgets. We also find that TAP's effectiveness depends on the attacker model, as both general capability and safety tuning affect attack success--stronger models produce more effective injections, while safety-tuned attackers can refuse to generate adversarial prompts. Task-universal attacks transfer effectively to unseen tasks and out-of-distribution domains, but attacks optimized on smaller open-source models do not transfer to frontier models like GPT-5. These findings highlight automated prompt injection a

_analysis.summary: This academic paper examines how automated prompt injection attacks—where malicious inputs trick AI systems into performing unintended actions—can exploit large language model (LLM) agents that interact with untrusted external data. The study finds that black-box optimization methods (which don’t rely on internal model details) are more effective than gradient-based attacks at bypassing safeguards in realistic agentic environments, tested across 80 tasks and four domains.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: Demonstrates a critical security and reliability vulnerability in AI agents that could undermine trust in AI-powered tools, potentially shifting competitive advantages among AI-first firms or creating new barriers to entry for smaller players.
_analysis.market_signal: AI agents vulnerable to prompt injection may face reputational and regulatory scrutiny, accelerating consolidation among firms with robust security infrastructure or slowing adoption of agentic systems in high-stakes markets.

items.title: A Deployment-Oriented Framework for Explainable AI-Assisted eBPF/XDP Mitigation at the IoT Edge
items.abstract: Internet of Things (IoT) deployments combine heterogeneous, resource-constrained devices with weak security configurations, exposed services, limited logging, patching constraints, and long lifecycles. Signature- and threshold-based controls remain useful baselines, but they are insufficient as standalone mechanisms in dynamic IoT networks. Likewise, offline artificial intelligence (AI) benchmark performance alone does not establish operational deployability. This article presents a conceptual framework and research agenda for a Linux-based IoT edge gateway that combines resource-aware flow-level AI-assisted risk scoring, event-level explainability, and bounded mitigation through eBPF/XDP. The controller applies reversible, time-limited actions subject to critical-device safeguards, updates packet-level enforcement state, and records structured logs. The architecture separates complex reasoning and policy control in user space from concise packet-handling decisions in the kernel. It also defines a future hardware-aware evaluation pathway covering detection quality, resource cost, response timing, rollback behaviour, and legitimate-traffic preservation. The paper does not report new

_analysis.summary: This academic paper proposes a new framework for using AI to improve cybersecurity at the edge of IoT (Internet of Things) networks, where devices are often outdated, poorly secured, and hard to update. It argues that traditional security methods are no longer enough and that AI must be designed to work efficiently on resource-limited devices to be practical.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: While this paper focuses on IoT security and AI deployment, it does not directly address digital competition policy, though IoT ecosystems can involve platform dynamics.
_analysis.market_signal: The paper signals growing demand for AI-driven security solutions in fragmented IoT markets, which could influence competition among cybersecurity and cloud providers.
key_entities: eBPF/XDP (Linux kernel technologies for network filtering)

items.title: HIPIF: Hierarchical Planning and Information Folding for Long-Horizon LLM Agent Learning
items.abstract: While Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated strong capabilities as autonomous agents across a wide range of tasks, their performance often degrades in multi-turn long-horizon agentic tasks. Existing methods have made progress through fine-grained credit assignment to alleviate long-horizon sparse rewards and hierarchical reinforcement learning to decompose tasks and reduce long-term dependency. However, these methods still do not directly address long-context interference, in which continuously growing histories weaken the agent's ability to track the global task state and impair subsequent reasoning and decision-making. Inspired by the way humans handle complex tasks through subgoal decomposition and completed progress summarization, we propose Hierarchical Planning and Information Folding (HIPIF) for long-horizon LLM agent learning. HIPIF trains the agent end-to-end to organize long-horizon execution around explicit subgoals while folding completed subgoal histories to reduce long-context interference. Furthermore, to stabilize subgoal-based planning and execution, HIPIF combines hierarchical reflection and subgoal-oriented process rewards to guide subgoal generation, tr

_analysis.summary: This paper introduces HIPIF, a method to improve how large language model (LLM) agents handle long, multi-step tasks by breaking them into smaller parts and managing the growing context of past actions. Current LLM agents struggle with long tasks because their performance drops as they process more information over time.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: This work signals a shift toward more capable AI agents that could disrupt digital markets by automating complex tasks currently dominated by human labor or specialized platforms.
_analysis.market_signal: Advances in LLM agent capabilities may accelerate automation in sectors like customer service, software development, and e-commerce, potentially reducing barriers to entry for new competitors.

items.title: MoE Enhanced Federated Learning for Spatiotemporal Prediction
items.abstract: Traffic prediction is fundamental to intelligent transportation systems and urban computing, yet many cities continue to suffer from traffic data scarcity due to limited sensor deployment and uneven urban development. Cross-city knowledge transfer has thus attracted increasing attention, enabling data-rich cities to assist data-scarce ones. However, centralized approaches raise privacy concerns, while existing federated methods struggle with pronounced spatiotemporal heterogeneity across cities. To address these challenges, we propose MoE-FedTP, a personalized federated cross-city spatiotemporal prediction framework based on lightweight Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) networks. MoE-FedTP first employs spatiotemporal neural networks to extract features from both source and target cities, then introduces a set of expert networks derived from different source cities through partial parameter sharing. A gating mechanism dynamically fuses the experts to capture diverse traffic dynamics, achieving fine-grained modeling of urban heterogeneity while preserving privacy. Experiments on four real-world traffic datasets show that MoE-FedTP consistently outperforms state-of-the-art cross-city and fede

_analysis.summary: This paper proposes MoE-FedTP, a federated learning (a decentralized AI training method where data stays on local devices) approach to improve traffic prediction across cities with limited data. It addresses challenges like privacy concerns and differences in traffic patterns between cities by using a 'Mixture of Experts' (MoE) model, which combines multiple specialized AI models to handle varied data.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: This paper highlights the growing importance of federated learning in addressing data scarcity and privacy in digital markets, which is relevant to competition policy debates on data access and platform power.
_analysis.market_signal: Federated learning could reduce reliance on centralized data monopolies, potentially disrupting traditional data aggregation models in digital markets.

items.title: A complementary study on PlanGPT: Evaluation with defined Performance Metrics and comparison with a planner
items.abstract: Automated Planning is a subfield of Artificial Intelligence (AI) where the main objective is generating a sequence of actions, known as a plan, that helps us reach a goal state from an initial state. A planning problem is defined by a set of objects, an initial state and a desired goal state. The objective is to compute a plan that'll lead us from the inital state to the goal state. Programs that generate plans are called planners. In this paper, we did a complementary study to the state-of-the-art LLM called PlanGPT which was released last year. We redid some experiments to verify whether planning with LLMs is \textbf{pertinent} and \textbf{worthwhile}. We also check whether the results obtained in the official PlanGPT paper for plan coverage were correct, and we also performed a more comprehensive study on PlanGPT's performance: in our paper PlanGPT's performance was evaluated using two metrics: Plan Cost and Plan Generation Time. The results of planGPT were compared to those produced by a traditional planner for the same plans and same metrics. We discovered that PlanGPT is no better than a Greedy search strategy.

_analysis.summary: This paper evaluates PlanGPT, an advanced AI system designed to generate sequences of actions (called 'plans') to achieve specific goals, by comparing it to traditional planning software. The study aims to validate PlanGPT's performance using defined metrics, focusing on its ability to solve complex problem-solving tasks in AI.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: While PlanGPT represents cutting-edge AI planning technology, it does not directly address digital market dynamics, competition policy, or platform power structures.
_analysis.market_signal: The rise of AI planning tools like PlanGPT signals potential future automation of complex tasks, which could indirectly influence digital market efficiency but does not immediately disrupt competitive dynamics.

items.title: Stop Early, Spend Less: Hidden-State Probes as a Practical Recipe for Streaming Moderation of LLM Outputs
items.abstract: Deploying large language models in user-facing systems requires efficient output safety filtering. Existing approaches typically rely on a separate moderation model applied after generation, which doubles inference cost and only detects violations after generation completes. We observe that the signal needed for moderation is already present in the model hidden states. Based on this, we train lightweight token-level probes that operate directly on internal activations, producing per-token safety scores that can be aggregated for both offline evaluation and online intervention. The probe reuses activations from the generator and requires no additional forward pass, enabling sub millisecond per-token safety checks inside the decoding loop. A probe applied to a single mid layer recovers most decisions of a strong guard model, acting as a low cost surrogate optimized for latency rather than accuracy. In streaming settings, it can halt or modify unsafe outputs before they are fully generated, replacing end of sequence moderation with continuous token level monitoring. Compared to post hoc and streaming guard models, our method achieves orders of magnitude lower compute overhead with min

_analysis.summary: This paper proposes a more efficient way to filter harmful content from large language models (LLMs) by analyzing their internal hidden states (the data the model generates as it processes text) rather than using a separate moderation tool. This approach reduces costs and allows for real-time safety checks during text generation, which could be important for companies deploying LLMs in user-facing products.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: This research highlights how AI safety innovations can reduce barriers to entry for smaller firms competing with dominant LLM providers, potentially reshaping market dynamics in the AI infrastructure space.
_analysis.market_signal: Efficient moderation tools could lower operational costs for LLM deployment, enabling more competition against established AI providers like OpenAI or Google.

items.title: Advancing the State-of-the-Art in Empirical Privacy Auditing
items.abstract: Parameter-efficient fine-tuning of large language models (LLMs) can exhibit problematic memorization of individual training examples. Empirical privacy auditing (EPA) quantifies this risk by measuring realistic data leakage on membership inference (MI) or reconstruction attacks. A key challenge in EPA is designing ``canary'' examples that are mixed with the privacy-sensitive training data. We propose generating synthetic canaries via high-temperature sampling ($T \geq 0.8$) from LLMs, using prompts tailored to the privacy-sensitive training data. These canaries act as high-influence outliers, ensuring high identifiability and hence strong audits. Further, since the canaries are themselves non-private, they are inspectable and can be inserted with repetition without jeopardizing the privacy of the real data. An important use of models fine-tuned on privacy-sensitive data is the generation of synthetic data. This also comes with privacy risk. We introduce a powerful synthetic data audit based on fine-tuning an auxiliary model on the synthetic data. Auditing the auxiliary model for the original canaries then provides a strong estimate of the privacy leakage through the synthetic data.

_analysis.summary: This paper introduces a new method for detecting when large language models (LLMs) memorize sensitive training data by inserting synthetic 'canary' examples (artificially generated test cases) into the training process. These canaries help measure how easily private information could leak during attacks like membership inference (where an attacker checks if a specific data point was in the training set) or reconstruction attacks (where an attacker tries to recreate private data).
_analysis.relevance_explanation: While not directly about competition policy, this research highlights risks in AI model training that could influence regulatory scrutiny over data monopolies and the market power of firms controlling vast datasets.
_analysis.market_signal: The paper signals growing concerns about data privacy risks in AI, which may push regulators to tighten rules on how dominant platforms handle user data, potentially affecting their competitive advantages.

items.title: ComBench: A Benchmark for Rigorous Proof Reasoning and Constructive Realization in Olympiad-Level Combinatorics
items.abstract: Combinatorics is central to Olympiad-level mathematical problem solving, requiring deep discrete reasoning, creative constructions, and rigorous structural insight. Recent evidence suggests that even today's strongest frontier models remain uneven on Olympiad combinatorics, revealing a gap in creative mathematical reasoning. We introduce ComBench, an Olympiad-level combinatorics benchmark for evaluating and diagnosing the combinatorial reasoning capabilities of large language models. ComBench contains 100 human-annotated competition-level problems organized around two complementary settings: analysis-centric problems, which primarily require rigorous mathematical arguments, and construction-centric problems, which require explicit constructions in addition to correctness justifications. The evaluation protocol combines rubric-guided proof grading with deterministic construction verification, exposing cases where proof quality and construction validity diverge. Experiments on frontier open- and closed-source models show that ComBench is far from saturated: the strongest model reaches 65.4% overall Avg. and 75.3% overall Best@4. We further find that Rigorous Proof Reasoning and Const

_analysis.summary: This paper introduces ComBench, a benchmark designed to test large language models (LLMs) on Olympiad-level combinatorics problems, which require deep discrete reasoning and creative problem-solving. The study finds that even advanced frontier models struggle with these tasks, highlighting a gap in AI's ability to perform high-level mathematical reasoning.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: While the paper focuses on AI's mathematical reasoning limitations, it indirectly signals the competitive dynamics of AI development markets, where firms with superior reasoning capabilities could gain an edge.
_analysis.market_signal: Advances in AI reasoning capabilities could disrupt the market for AI-powered problem-solving tools, potentially benefiting firms that achieve breakthroughs in combinatorial reasoning.

items.title: Decoupling Thought from Speech: Knowledge-Grounded Counterfactual Reasoning for Resilient Multi-Agent Argumentation
items.abstract: Multi-agent debate frameworks have been shown to improve large language model performance in convergent tasks, but they are currently optimized in a way that heavily favors final output accuracy rather than stability of the process. During long-horizon exchanges reactive systems under sustained perturbations often experience logic degradation, argument repetition, and role drift. To structurally prevent the identity loss and maintain the process fidelity, we introduce Knowledge-Grounded Counterfactual Reasoning (KG-CFR), a dual-stage architecture that enforces a strict separation of concerns between a private, retrieval-augmented planning buffer, and a public execution layer. We assess this system in Dynamic Resource Allocation under Uncertainty (DRAU), a dedicated 1v1v1 environment, introducing diversity as distinct from standard debate settings. Over 270 completely factorial crisis simulation trajectories with stochastic environmental shocks, KG-CFR prevents judge-detected critical post-shock degradation (defined as a quality shift, $Δ\le -0.20$) in more than 95% of perturbed runs, increasing the overall argument quality from 0.694 to 0.822. Our primary contribution is the demons

_analysis.summary: This paper introduces Knowledge-Grounded Counterfactual Reasoning (KG-CFR), a new method to improve the stability of multi-agent debate frameworks using large language models (LLMs). By separating the reasoning process from the output generation, KG-CFR aims to prevent issues like logic degradation and role drift during long discussions.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: While not directly about competition policy, this paper highlights how AI systems can maintain coherence in multi-agent interactions, which may influence the design of AI-driven platforms and their market dynamics.
_analysis.market_signal: AI systems capable of stable, multi-agent reasoning could enable new forms of competitive AI-driven services, potentially disrupting existing platform dominance.

items.title: Detecting Speculative Language in Biomedical Texts using Recurrent Neural Tensor Networks
items.abstract: In this investigation, we delve into the automated detection of speculative language within biomedical articles by utilizing distributed sentence representations and advanced deep learning techniques. The implications of such identification extend to information retrieval, multi-document summarization, and the exploration of new knowledge. Our exploration encompasses two distinct approaches for acquiring distributed sentence representations: the Paragraph Vector model and the Recursive Neural Tensor Network. These methodologies are then rigorously compared against three foundational baseline algorithms: Support Vector Machines, Naive Bayes, and pattern matching. Our findings reveal that the Recursive Neural Tensor Network (RNTN) demonstrates a slight performance edge (F1 = 0.885) over the top-performing baseline, the linear bigram SVM (F1 = 0.881). Meanwhile, the Paragraph Vector model proves less effective (F1 = 0.368), even after extensive training using an expansive, unlabeled dataset. We engage in a comprehensive discourse on the factors influencing these performance disparities and provide insightful recommendations for future research directions.

_analysis.summary: This academic paper explores how to automatically detect speculative language (uncertain or conditional statements) in biomedical research articles using advanced deep learning techniques like the Recursive Neural Tensor Network and Paragraph Vector model. These methods could improve information retrieval and knowledge discovery in scientific literature.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: While this paper focuses on biomedical text analysis, its techniques could indirectly influence digital competition policy by improving content moderation or misinformation detection in platform ecosystems.
_analysis.market_signal: This signals advancements in natural language processing that could enhance AI-driven content analysis tools used by digital platforms to manage misinformation or speculative claims.

items.title: ERAlign: Energy-based Representation Alignment of GNNs and LLMs on Text-attributed Graphs
items.abstract: Text-attributed Graphs (TAGs) incorporate textual node attributes with graph structures to describe rich relational semantics. Recent efforts to integrate Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) and Large Language Models (LLMs) have shown promise for learning on TAGs, yet achieving well-aligned representations remains challenging. Prior studies largely rely on heuristics that perform coarse-grained matching. They lack sufficient constraints and ignore distributional alignment, leading to representation drift and limited generalization. Building on Energy-based Models (EBMs), we propose an Energy-based Representation Alignment (ERAlign) framework that projects GNN-encoded graph structure and LLM-derived text embeddings in a shared latent space to achieve distribution consistency. Concretely, layer-wise alignment is quantified by a distance metric and optimized via an EBM objective. By decreasing energy values, our framework yields well-aligned representations for downstream tasks. During training, we introduce Energy Discrepancy (ED) to avoid high sampling costs associated with intractable normalization. ED also carries theoretical guarantees of higher training efficiency and reduced energy la

_analysis.summary: This academic paper introduces ERAlign, a new method to better align the representations (how data is numerically encoded) of Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) and Large Language Models (LLMs) when working with text-attributed graphs (TAGs). TAGs combine text data with network structures, like social media connections or citation networks. The authors argue that existing approaches fail to properly align these representations, leading to poor generalization and inconsistent results.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: While not directly about competition policy, this paper highlights advancements in AI integration that could strengthen the capabilities of dominant digital platforms or enable new entrants to challenge them by improving AI-driven graph-based services.
_analysis.market_signal: Improved AI integration in graph-based systems may accelerate the dominance of platforms with large-scale graph data (e.g., social networks, search engines) or enable new competitors to leverage such data more effectively.

items.title: Data-Driven Automation
items.abstract: We build a dynamic model of data-driven automation in which data (i) is heterogeneous and task-specific; (ii) accumulates endogenously as a byproduct of economic activity; and (iii) exhibits spillovers such that data generated by one task can augment the productivity of another. Along the transition path of automation, data plays a dual role in simultaneously augmenting the productivity of already-automated tasks and expanding the automation frontier. We derive tight conditions for the economy to be partially versus fully automated in the long-run. In the latter case, automation exhibits rich short-run dynamics that depend on the pattern of data spillovers but is always slow in the long-run: the share of tasks produced by labor decays asymptotically as a power law in time. We show that the economy is generically inefficient and analyze how a planner optimally tilts the direction of data accumulation. With endogenous capital accumulation, data-driven automation generates explosive growth but stagnant long-run wages.

_analysis.summary: This paper models how data-driven automation (where data improves productivity in tasks and can be reused across tasks) shapes economic growth and automation over time. It finds that economies may end up fully or partially automated depending on how data accumulates and spreads, with data spillovers playing a key role in expanding automation possibilities.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: It reveals how data accumulation and spillovers can create winner-takes-all dynamics in digital markets, reinforcing the market power of firms that control large datasets.
_analysis.market_signal: Data-rich firms may gain disproportionate advantages in automation, potentially entrenching their dominance in digital markets.

items.title: Duality for Optimal Multi-Item, Multi-Bidder Auction Design: Revenue Certificates through Deep Learning
items.abstract: Characterizing revenue-optimal auctions for multi-item, multi-bidder settings remains a fundamental open problem, with no known closed-form solution existing beyond restrictive binary-type instances. This has motivated interest in computational approaches to optimal auction design. In this paper, we introduce the first computational framework that directly tackles the dual problem for multi-item, multi-bidder auctions and dominant-strategy incentive compatibility (DSIC), generating certified revenue upper bounds. Our approach parametrizes Lagrange multipliers with a structurally guaranteed strict flow-conservation property using neural networks, enabling efficient optimization over feasible dual solutions via gradient descent. To bridge the gap between discrete computational methods and theoretical guarantees for continuous types, we develop a novel lifting technique that maps dual certificates from coarse discretizations to fine refinements. We prove that lifting gives valid revenue upper bounds for multi-item, multi-bidder auctions with continuous uniform valuations. Furthermore, we give a generalized lifting construction for arbitrary continuous distributions and demonstrate tha

_analysis.summary: This paper presents a new computational method to design optimal auctions for selling multiple items to multiple bidders, a long-standing challenge in auction theory. By solving the dual problem of auction design, it generates revenue upper bounds that could help regulators evaluate whether digital platforms are extracting excessive profits through their auction mechanisms.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: While not directly about competition policy, the paper's insights into optimal auction design could inform regulators' understanding of how digital platforms (e.g., ad exchanges) structure their revenue-maximizing mechanisms, potentially revealing exploitative practices.
_analysis.market_signal: Advances in computational auction design may accelerate the automation of pricing strategies by digital platforms, intensifying concerns about algorithmic collusion or unfair revenue extraction.

items.title: Decision-Making under Combinatorial Risk
items.abstract: Decision-making under risk is typically studied through single-shot lottery choices. Yet many real decisions involve combinatorial risk, where risk arises from multiple risky components, so the lottery over outcomes is induced rather than given outright and can be costly to evaluate exactly. We introduce an investment-allocation task to study decision under combinatorial risk, where investing in a component raises its success probability and thereby reshapes the outcome distribution. Participants favor the option with the larger probability increment, and, when increments are equal, the option with the higher initial success probability. Revealing the induced probability mass function (PMF) substantially changes behavior, making participants less responsive to combinatorial-risk features and reducing choice variance. To explain these patterns, we move beyond standard benchmarks and hand-crafted hypotheses with symbolic regression to discover compact descriptive models. The discovered models rely mainly on combinatorial-risk features, such as the after-investment success probability, rather than exact evaluation of the full induced distribution. Behavior under the displayed PMF is t

_analysis.summary: This paper studies how people make decisions when facing combinatorial risk—situations where multiple risky choices interact to shape the final outcome. In an investment-allocation task, participants preferred options that offered the largest increase in success probability, even when the overall outcome distribution was complex to evaluate.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: While not directly about digital markets, the paper’s insights into how decision-makers evaluate complex, interdependent risks could inform models of platform strategy and consumer behavior in digital ecosystems.
_analysis.market_signal: The study highlights how users may simplify decision-making in multi-component digital markets (e.g., app ecosystems or ad auctions), potentially influencing competitive dynamics.

items.title: Introduction to gravity model for beginners
items.abstract: This paper provides a didactic and beginner friendly review of the gravity model and its conceptual translation from classical physics into international economics. After a brief introduction, it begins by establishing the structural and mathematical parallels between Newton's law of universal gravitation and the economic gravity equation, demonstrating how economic mass (GDP) attracts trade flows while geographic distance acts as a source of spatial resistance. The paper then examines the deterministic philosophical framework required to apply rigid natural laws to collective human behavior. Finally, it traces the chronological evolution of the literature, highlighting the historical divergence between the physics rooted approach developed by early demographers and Walter Isard and the utility based econometric adaptations that later emerged. The paper ultimately shows that, despite globalization, spatial friction remains a significant and measurable force shaping international trade and geopolitical interactions.

_analysis.summary: This paper explains the gravity model, which compares economic trade flows to Newton's law of gravity, where economic mass (like GDP) attracts trade and geographic distance acts as a barrier. It discusses the challenges of applying rigid natural laws to human economic behavior.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: While the gravity model is foundational in trade economics, it does not directly address digital markets or competition policy.
_analysis.market_signal: The paper signals no direct market development in digital competition, as it focuses on traditional international trade theory.

items.title: The Dispossessed: Large-Scale Land Acquisitions, Elite Capture, and Dissent in Africa
items.abstract: Over the past two decades, millions of hectares of land in Africa have been transferred to investors, raising fears of displacement and conflict. This paper estimates the causal impact of large-scale land acquisitions (LSLAs) on local dissent by comparing successfully implemented projects to a control group of exogenously failed deals. Using staggered difference-in-differences estimators across 1,391 geocoded deals, I find that LSLAs cause a sustained increase in civic unrest of 158% relative to the pre-treatment mean.
